reviews.review_text:
Spore is one of those games that still feels unique even today. Few titles have ever attempted something this ambitious: letting players guide a species from microscopic organism to intergalactic civilization, all while giving them creative freedom at nearly every stage of the journey.

The gameplay constantly evolves. Each phase introduces different mechanics and styles of play, from survival and evolution to strategy, exploration, and space simulation. Not every stage is equally deep, but the sheer variety keeps the experience fresh and unpredictable.

What truly made Spore legendary, though, was its creation system. Even today, it is genuinely impressive how much freedom the tools give you. Creatures, vehicles, buildings, spaceships… players managed to recreate or adapt almost anything imaginable. The community creations alone became a huge part of the experience and are still fascinating to look at years later.

There is something almost infinite about the game. You can spend countless hours experimenting, evolving species, exploring planets, or simply creating bizarre monstrosities for fun. It captures a sense of creativity and discovery that very few games manage to replicate.

Of course, some mechanics can feel simplified or shallow compared to fully dedicated games in those genres, and the space stage especially can become repetitive over time. But honestly, it is hard to hold that too much against a game trying to do this many things at once.

Spore remains one of the most original and imaginative games ever made. A true classic of creative sandbox gaming.

Score: 9/10 – A wonderfully creative and endlessly fascinating experience powered by one of the best creation systems ever designed.

Bro my game was bugging out and in tribal stage which made the towns become submerged under water... anyway I started a new playthrough and my og species turned up in the following tribal stage. Holy reunion!
Kinda falls off after the creature stage for me. Wish that the game had more content in the creature and cell stages.

good but really limited, and don't but the dlcs theyr's just not good, had a bit of fun but it's really just kinda ok-tier, still recomend in general tho, mostly if you can't buy stellaris an it's 50 dlcs

Spore is such an easy game to get lost in, every time I start making new species of creatures it takes me back to the GameBoy and DS. All of the stages of life are super fun although my favorite stages are the mutation and civilization stages. Once you've played through enough it's really fun to have all of your different species show up together on the same planet and watch natural selection choose which ones are strong enough to thrive with your newest creations. It can be a fun roleplaying experience.
There's a lot of things to do. Spore is an oldie but a goodie. Definitely worth the price.
The music is nice while building characters and playing. This is a fun time!

The game from my childhood, it's still a lot of fun even though it has aged a little poorly, but it's a great game to introduce to a child. There are some really good roleplaying elements within the game, and the space level is my favorite.

I wish I had known about this game back in 2008! I absolutely LOVE classic Maxis games, as I grew up playing them and have many originals still with crisp boxes stored away. If the name of the game had been something a bit more "creature builder" oriented, I think I would have had this game decades ago. Thank goodness for Steam and the search feature, as I have swiftly become obsessed with this creature / city / space building game. The game is full of funny quips, conversations, creatures, and has tons of references to many other classic games and movies throughout it. I wish it spent more time on creature > tribal stages, but all around I give the game an A+.
amazing game gets very boring at space exploration stage

I find myself playing this on and off because its a very unique sort of game where you can build your own little species and civilization from the cell stage, and I have a lot of fun doing it at least for a little while, like most older games its prone to some bugs and some things don't work as well as you'd like and you have to find odd little workarounds, make sure to save often as its also prone to crash and you'll lose your progress, I think its worth trying at least once and there is definitely something good still to be said about how fun and unique it is even as old as it is
One of my favourite older games, big part of my childhood. Peak game, 10/10.

7/10

Thanks to the wonders of EA and how the game hasn't had any recent major updates, it simply can't compete with its competitors anymore. Even though the core concepts are good, I simply can't rate it higher.

Spore. Best game ever created. The only reason it was taken down is because the effect it had on this world was too dangerous. The Creature Creator was never a toy. It was a test. Millions of people sat down and, without being told how, intuitively designed functioning organisms — spines that bore weight, jaws that articulated, limbs that balanced. Children were doing in twenty minutes what evolutionary biologists spend careers modeling. Somewhere in a quiet office, someone realized what that meant. The human brain, given the right interface, could engineer life. And every save file was a blueprint. Then came the Space stage. People didn't play it like a game. They played it like gods. They terraformed. They wiped out civilizations for resources. They uplifted primitive species into slaves. They did it casually, between snacks, while half-watching TV. The game wasn't teaching us how to build worlds. It was revealing what we'd do once we could. The data was the real product. Every creature uploaded to the Sporepedia, every empire conquered, every planet scoured — all of it feeding something. A model of human creative instinct paired with human moral restraint, and the ratio was catastrophic. Whoever was reading those numbers saw what was coming. We were one generation of tools away from doing it for real. Designer organisms. Engineered ecosystems. Whole biospheres built by people who learned ethics from a cartoon UFO. They didn't pull Spore because the sequels flopped. They pulled it because the pipeline was already forming — biotech labs hiring kids who'd grown up sculpting genomes for fun, CRISPR kits showing up in classrooms, AI tools starting to generate proteins the way Spore generated spines. The game was the dress rehearsal. And the audience was learning its lines too well. Somewhere, the servers are still archived. The creatures are still there, frozen mid-evolution, waiting. And every year, the gap between what Spore let us imagine and what the real world lets us build gets a little smaller. They didn't take it down to end the game. They took it down to delay the ending it was writing for us. And we're catching up to it anyway.

I love this game so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ much man! if you like character customization, this is actually your game, all 8 hours I have in this game (at the time of making this review) were the day I got it and making creatures in the Creator Mode, definitely worth Twenty Bucks!

My only real criticisms of this game is that the stages before you get to space feel too short, even in hard mode. The game can crash without warning or reason, which will cause me to to step away and never play the game for months out of frustration.

the game is almost 20 and they haven't reduced the price since then so, like... you should really only buy it on sale.
but! if you haven't experienced Spore yet, there's no other game like it, and there most likely never will be. although i appreciate the valiant attempts at 'spiritual successors', this kind of game (well, really, it's a one-of-a-kind game) demands a lot of resources to develop that indie teams just don't have (and obviously big studios don't want to invest so much into risky projects when they can release what they know will turn a profit. not to be a downer, lol.)
Spore is just a really weird and unique game that i love dearly. for all of its flaws and jank, i just can't help but love it because, like. what a wacky concept.? it is so full of whimsy and endless creative potential. it's kinda like Minecraft in that way; its a game you can keep coming back to over and over for years on end.
i also constantly praise the art direction... Will Wright described the designs of the creatures as 'falling somewhere between Pixar and Giger', and it really works. the soundtrack for this game is also very much overlooked. there are some more structured songs, like the Sporepedia music (by Saul Stokes) and the library of Galactic Adventures songs, but almost all other music in the game is dynamically composed. it responds to user input very subtly and never distracts from the experience -- but when it's not there, it's sorely missed! there's very few soundtracks in gaming that pull this off so well. (big ups to Brian Eno!)

i strongly believe its a game that anyone can enjoy. there's no right or wrong way to play. it can be cosy on easy mode, genuinely cruel on hard mode, and most people will comfortably enjoy the full normal mode campaign. plus it doesn't matter if you want to make masterpieces or walking phalluses, whether you want to be peaceful or violent. ultimately Spore was designed to let the player create whatever story, worlds, and abominations they want, so there's something in it for everyone.

It certainly isn't for everyone but the creation aspect of this game, the vibe and especially the space stage have a certain way of resonating with that childlike wonder and curiosity part of one's heart.

WOOOOOOW

I've wanted to play this game for years, ever since i first saw a Youtuber playing it. Now that I finally have it for myself, it's everything I could have hoped for. The creature creator is wacky and can be used to make some real monstrosities, and in the Civilization stage, I *highly* recommend building the most unethically evil weapon you possibly can.

It's exactly what I thought it would be a simple, fun, easy game about evolution

I'd give this game a neutral review if Steam gave the option. 
The creature creator is very fun to play with --- you can really spend a long time making some wacky stuff , and it's really cool that you can spot said stuff in the wild during later playthroughs. The microorganism and land stages of your creature's existence are pretty fun for me, but I'd say the rest is pretty tedious. Even then, they're both pretty basic. Once you've played it once or twice you've experienced everything it has to offer. The fun parts of this game are done a lot better in other games, and if you're here to make creatures I'd recommend drawing/3d modeling one yourself or searching for another creature creator to play with.
This game is so good, even in 2026 ,spore is creature evolution simulation and it's so good i can't describe how good it is.
I recommend this game as the ultimate god sim for evolution theory nerds like me! the creature stage is one of the most fun stages ever. the tribal stage sucks though.

Enslave the people who surrender to you and make them work in brutal working conditions. Don't place down entertainment buildings (not good for spice production). Happiness comes second for other alien races. (If at all). Feel the glory your ancestors felt when they sailed to ocean to make money at other lesser people's expense. Ally to "snatched waist empire" and have them serve as your personal femboy concubine.

Become rich. Become powerful. Become great.

Become god.
